lesson: The # (h1) heading is Slide 1. The intro paragraph immediately below it is the slide body. <!-- Slide 1 --> goes after that paragraph, before the first --- separator. Miscounting here leads to slide total mismatches.
A short recap slide (≤2 min) bridging from the previous lecture's limitations works better than jumping straight into new syntax. It gives students a motivation frame before content starts.

The instructor's PDF uses questions as slide titles (e.g. 'What's the big idea?', 'That sounds like an if statement…'). Apply this question-as-hook pattern more consistently on introductory slides — it mirrors the source material's style and pulls students in.

When a lecture introduces multiple variants of the same concept (e.g. three loop types), end with a 'which one to use?' decision framework before the lab. Students need synthesis before application — skipping it produces guessing, not reasoning.

The intro paragraph under a topic's # heading should appear on a separate ## slide with the same title, not on the # slide itself. In Reveal.js the # slide is a vertical navigation parent — putting content there makes it easy to skip.

When a slide needs both a concept explanation and a code example, use two consecutive ## slides with the same title: first slide has the concept as bullets, second slide has the code. This is the implementation of the 'no text on both sides of code' rule.

Use ::: {.columns} / ::: {.column width="50%"} / ::: / ::: for two-column layout. Code blocks inside columns require ```{.cpp} (dot notation) not ```cpp. Close each column with ::: and the outer container with ::: (trailing space). Use for side-by-side code comparison and multi-choice scenario slides.

When you have a code example do not have text on the same page unless the example is very short (<6 lines). Even then, have text either above or below the code example but not both. It’s OK to add another slide if needed.
Prefer lists to blocks of text.
